title: Vietnamese Chicken Salad

overview.description: A fresh Vietnamese salad of tender shredded chicken, crunchy cabbage, carrot, herbs and roasted peanuts dressed with lime, fish sauce and a touch of sweetness.

ingredients: ½ small white cabbage, finely shredded
1 small red onion, very thinly sliced
1 large handful fresh coriander, roughly chopped
1 large handful fresh mint leaves, roughly chopped
1 small red chilli, finely sliced, optional
method: Place the chicken in a saucepan and cover with cold water.
Bring the water gently to a simmer.
Cook until the chicken is completely cooked through.
Remove the chicken and allow it to cool.
Shred the chicken into thin strips.
Place the shredded cabbage, carrot and red onion in a large bowl.
Add the coriander, mint and spring onions.
Whisk together the lime juice, fish sauce, sugar, water and garlic.
Stir until the sugar has dissolved.
Add the shredded chicken to the vegetables.
Pour over most of the dressing.
Taste and adjust the balance of lime, fish sauce and sugar.
Scatter over the peanuts just before serving.

learning.did-you-know: Gỏi gà is a Vietnamese shredded chicken salad whose name simply means chicken salad. Different regions and households use varying combinations of cabbage, herbs, onion, Vietnamese coriander and other fresh ingredients.
learning.what-you-will-learn: How to combine tender meat with raw crunchy vegetables and how to balance the Vietnamese flavour combination of sweet, sour, salty and aromatic ingredients.

notes.why-its-in-the-chapman-kitchen: A complete meal salad that uses familiar chicken in a completely different format, while introducing the children to the fresh herbs, lime and fish sauce flavours of Vietnamese cooking.
chapman-tips: Chicken thighs stay particularly juicy, but breasts work well too.
Don't overcook the chicken; it should remain tender enough to shred easily.
The dressing should taste bold on its own because the cabbage will dilute it slightly.
Add the peanuts at the end so they remain crunchy.
Vietnamese coriander is excellent if you can find it, but regular coriander and mint make a good combination.
For a family version, leave the chilli out of the main bowl and add it to adult portions.
The salad benefits from a short resting time but should not sit dressed for hours.
If using leftover cooked chicken, this becomes a very quick meal.
notes.batch-cooking: The chicken can be cooked ahead, but the salad is best assembled and dressed shortly before serving.
notes.leftovers: Best eaten within 1 day. Leftovers can be used as a filling for rice paper rolls or sandwiches.
